coredata.dc:title: Superhydrophilic 3D-printed scaffolds using conjugated bioresorbable nanocomposites for enhanced bone regeneration
coredata.dc:creator: Dave, Khyati
Mahmud, Zaheri
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description: Scaffolds synthesized from synthetic polymers, though mechanically robust, are hydrophobic in nature, lack sufficient cell attracting cues and are not amenable to monitoring. These drawbacks, however, can be overcome by fabricating a new generation of biocompatible scaffolds with quantum nanoparticle additives and surface functionalization. The objective of the present work was to investigate the enhancement of cellular adhesion, growth, proliferation, differentiation and migration on polymer-based scaffolds. To achieve this, carbon dots (CDs) were melt blended with poly(L-Lactide) acid or PLA, followed by rapid prototyping to generate photoluminescent scaffolds with the advantages of hydrophilicity and built-in monitoring opportunity via two-photon microscopy. Apart from these, cell proliferation and migration aspects in the biological environment are found to be enhanced by oxygen plasma treatment which enables polymer superhydrophilicity. Carbon dots introduces nanoscale roughness, and plasma incorporates hydrophilic oxygen and hydroxyl groups on the scaffolds. After a 7 min plasma treatment, PLA-CD surfaces were found to gain superhydrophilicity (contact angle of 0˚) and cell proliferation increased by 70% relative to the untreated scaffold. Quantitative analysis and bioimaging showed significant increases in ECM mineralization. Thus, the plasma treated PLA-CD composite supports the potential for producing high-performance, monitorable scaffolds for tissue engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polylactic acid
Quantum dots
Scaffolds
Polymer composites
Oxygen plasma

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical characterization: FTIR and XPS measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Plasma treatment and its effects on composite scaffold.
Contact angle (CA) for PLA-CD surface after argon plasma treatment (t = exposure time; values shown are averages of 3 replicates).
Contact angle (CA) for PLA-CD surface after oxygen plasma treatment (t = exposure time; values shown are averages of 3 replicates).
Images of plasma treated PLA and PLA-CD films with variable exposure time intervals.

A) XPS full survey spectra of PLA-CD before oxygen plasma treatment, (B) XPS full survey spectra of PLA-CD after oxygen plasma treatment, (C) XPS high-resolution C1s peak fitting of PLA-CD before oxygen plasma treatment, (D) XPS high-resolution C1s peak fitting of PLA-CD after oxygen plasma treatment.
Saos-2 cell proliferation on PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P (t = 7 min) scaffolds evaluated over 4, 8 and 12 days of cell culture.
Results from intracellular alkaline phosphatase studies in Saos-2 cells cultured on PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P scaffolds (values averaged from 3 replicates).
Calcium deposits of Saos-2 cells cultured on scaffolds for weeks 3 and 4 (from 3 replicates) Mineralization was assessed using Alizarin red staining.
Effect of argon and oxygen plasma treatment on measured contact angles.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: CA = contact angle; t = plasma treatment duration; –a: there was no plasma treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface roughness (Rrms) of PLA and PLA-CD with variable exposure time intervals.
Elemental composition of PLA-CD before and after oxygen plasma treatment.
XPS high-resolution C1s peak deconvolution results before and after oxygen plasma treatment.

head.ce:title: Superhydrophilic 3D-printed scaffolds using conjugated bioresorbable nanocomposites for enhanced bone regeneration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Institute, The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nano Institute, The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scaffolds synthesized from synthetic polymers, though mechanically robust, are hydrophobic in nature, lack sufficient cell attracting cues and are not amenable to monitoring. These drawbacks, however, can be overcome by fabricating a new generation of biocompatible scaffolds with quantum nanoparticle additives and surface functionalization. The objective of the present work was to investigate the enhancement of cellular adhesion, growth, proliferation, differentiation and migration on polymer-based scaffolds. To achieve this, carbon dots (CDs) were melt blended with poly(L-Lactide) acid or PLA, followed by rapid prototyping to generate photoluminescent scaffolds with the advantages of hydrophilicity and built-in monitoring opportunity via two-photon microscopy. Apart from these, cell proliferation and migration aspects in the biological environment are found to be enhanced by oxygen plasma treatment which enables polymer superhydrophilicity. Carbon dots introduces nanoscale roughness, and plasma incorporates hydrophilic oxygen and hydroxyl groups on the scaffolds. After a 7 min plasma treatment, PLA-CD surfaces were found to gain superhydrophilicity (contact angle of 0˚) and cell proliferation increased by 70% relative to the untreated scaffold. Quantitative analysis and bioimaging showed significant increases in ECM mineralization. Thus, the plasma treated PLA-CD composite supports the potential for producing high-performance, monitorable scaffolds for tissue engineering.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Plasma treated poly(lactic acid) and carbon dot composite
STereoLithography file format family or Standard Triangle Language

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing rates of bone defects due to aging, osteoporosis, arthritis, bone cancer and related diseases have generated tremendous interest in bone tissue engineering. To preclude failures, there has been growing demands on scaffolds to be mechanically robust, biocompatible, bioactive, osteoconductive and biodegradable [1,2]. For bone defects, instead of placing invasive titanium plates in the body, a bone repair can be better promoted using the implantation of non-metal scaffolds [3]. Synthetic polymers such as poly(lactic acid), poly(caprolactone), poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid), poly(urethane) and poly(hydroxybutyrate) are often used in bone regeneration studies; however, their biomedical application is limited due to their reduced bioactivity and hydrophobicity [1].

Properties of polymers or biomaterials can be enhanced by incorporating fillers such as nanomaterials in the polymer matrices [4]. Several studies demonstrated that nanomaterials combined with ceramics, biomaterials or polymers promote cellular adhesion and differentiation [2,5]. Quantum dots can be incorporated with natural and synthetic polymers to produce hybrid nanocomposite polymers. Such hybrid polymer matrices with nanoparticles can have materials with enhanced toughness and strength [6,7]. The novel approach of integrating CDs with polymers for tissue engineering is a promising one. The application of inorganic and organic nanoparticles to produce nanocomposite scaffolds for characterizations have been reported. For example, PCL/AgNps filaments used to create customised 3D structures were found to be cytocompatible in hFOB cells and to have antibacterial action with E.coli. The materials were noted as suitable for bone tissue engineering due to their interconnected pore structure, mechanical and antimicrobial capabilities [8]. PLA-based scaffolds with graphene oxide (GO) reinforcements, fabricated by fused deposition modelling, were found to promote cell proliferation and mineralization more effectively than pure PLA scaffolds. The cell viability, attachment, proliferation, and differentiation studies were performed using MG-63 osteosarcoma cells [9]. CDs have attracted interest for potential applications in drug delivery, energy storage, catalysis, bioimaging and therapeutics. Their properties of nontoxicity, biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence, facile surface functionalization account for their versatile applications [10]. Their surface roughness due to nanoscale structures affects wettability and initial protein adsorption through changes in surface energy [11]. These attributes favour enhanced integrin binding, leading to increased cellular proliferation, spreading and ECM formation [12].

Traditional scaffold fabrication techniques (gas foaming, particle leaching, electrospinning, solvent casting, phase separation) do not provide sufficient freedom to control scaffold structures, pore geometry, porosity and nanoscale roughness. Modern engineering techniques based on additive manufacturing have overcome traditional scaffold fabrication methodologies. These technologies provide greater architectural control, reproducibility and adaptability to engineer site specific requirements by mimicking native tissue requirements [13]. Scaffold fabrication techniques play a major role in the final structure and biological response of implanted biomaterials. Efficient fabrication methods can enable synthesis of bespoke composite scaffolds possessing desirable porosity, pore interconnectivity and osteoconductivity, which can then support effective tissue engineering [14]. An interesting study reported the use of bioactive glass nanoparticles within polycaprolactone scaffolds. The scaffold pore surface was activated by oxygen plasma to facilitate simvastatin (SIM) immobilization. The designed release profile demonstrated that the plasma treatment can be used to regulate the release of SIM from the scaffolds. MG-63 cell adhesion, viability, alkaline phosphatase activity and the biomarker osteocalcin were thus enhanced [15].

3D printing facilitates the design of scaffolds through precise control of their design parameters. Scaffolds can be customized by optimizing process parameters such as scaffold geometry, strut height, width, number of layers, layer height, in-fill, pore size and interconnectivity, etc. New bone development is achieved by employing 3D bioactive scaffolds that serve as template matrices for cellular growth and proliferation. A challenge encountered in tissue engineering is to fabricate 3D constructs with appropriate pore size, surface properties and mechanical strength to allow cell adhesion over polymer-based scaffolds [7,16].

Synthetic polymers such as poly(lactic acid) or PLA, often employed in bone regeneration due to their mechanical strength, are hydrophobic in nature which restricts cellular activities. To reduce polymer hydrophobicity, surface treatments such as functional agents, coatings and similar protocols are often indicated [12]. Chemical treatments often pose the risk of leaching, while the coating of scaffolds can lead to non-uniform coverage and thickness. Plasma treatment is one approach that can potentially resolve these problems to enhance scaffold surface chemistry by generating binding sites for improved immobilization of proteins, drugs and biomolecules [17]. Plasma treatment is also known to modify the scaffold surface composition and nanoscale roughness [11]. Studies have shown improvements in polymer properties after oxygen plasma treatment, evidenced by enhanced cell proliferation [13] and mimicking of the natural ECM microenvironment [14]. Thus, surface modification by oxygen plasma treatment can render the surface hydrophilic by oxidation of the surface while generating chemically reactive or oxygen-bearing functional groups [18].

One major drawback of working with synthetic biopolymers is its hydrophobicity, as a result, there are insufficient cell recognition signals. The application of oxygen plasma to the surface of 3D printed PLA scaffolds is a facile method for improving PLA hydrophilicity and surface roughness. By replacing hydrogen with oxygen-containing species, plasma treatments can convert low-energy surfaces to higher-energy ones and improve surface wettability by forming carboxyl, hydroxyl and similar groups to boost scaffold hydrophilicity, depending on the plasma type and operating conditions. Although plasma treatment of PLA and other biocompatible polymers have been investigated previously, there is a dearth of results on the effects of plasma treatment on PLA nanocomposites. In particular, the synergistic effects of plasma and nanoparticles integrated into PLA are yet to be reported. The significant functionalizing potential and generation of surface nanoroughness by a combination of both carbon quantum dots (CDs) and plasma treatment on PLA scaffolds remain to be investigated. Additional challenges include considerations of compatibility between the hydrophobic PLA and the typically hydrophilic CDs, optimizing the nanocomposite formulation and the treatment protocol.

We developed hydrophilic and biocompatible composite scaffolds by combining bioresorbable, hydrophobic PLA polymer conjugated with amphiphilic nanomaterial followed by cold plasma treatment. The PLA pellets were first melt blended with multispectral carbon quantum dots (CDs), extruded into filaments followed by 3D printing into PLA-CD scaffolds and treatment with oxygen plasma. The resulting scaffolds were found to possess enhanced surface hydrophilicity relative to non-treated PLA and PLA-CD composites. The plasma-treated PLA-CD (PLA-CD@P) scaffolds were investigated for their cell adhesion, cell proliferation, osteogenic differentiation and extracellular matrix mineralization. The process is shown schematically in Fig. 1 .

The materials used in the study include β-glycerophosphate (≥99% (titration), CAS number 154804–51-0), L-ascorbic acid ((≥98%, CAS number 50–81-7), DAPI (≥98%, CAS number 28718–90-3), Trypsin (CAS number 9002–07-7), ALP (P7998, ready to use), ARS (CAS number 130–22-3).

The biodegradable, biocompatible and bioresorbable poly(L-Lactide) or PLA samples (RESOMER®, L210S) were obtained from Evonik, Germany (MW: 311 kDa; inherent viscosity = 3.3–4.3 dL/g). The PLA pellets were ground and melted at 215 0C for melt-blending with carbon quantum dots (CDs). The CDs were synthesized from curcumin precursor [19], and 0.1 wt% CD was blended with the PLA. The composite thus prepared was found to be adequate for their monitoring purposes (Fig. S1-S4, ESI).

The PLA-CD blends were extruded into filaments of 1.75 mm diameter, suitable for 3D printing. These filaments were used in conjunction with the Makerbot Replicator 2 for FDM-based (fused deposition modelling) 3D printing of composite (PLA-CD) scaffolds. The scaffold geometry was selected based on several criteria: to compare with past studies conducted in the field [20-23] in order to assess the efficacy/advancement with our method; to provide proof-of-concept with this first attempt with the PLA-CD@P; to meet the requirements of porosity and pore interconnectivity for exchanges across the scaffold [12]; and, microplate well dimensions available for cell culture studies.

Porous scaffolds having a length of 10.5 mm, a width of 10.5 mm and a depth of 1 mm were designed using SolidWorks CAD software. The height, width and length of the individual struts within the scaffold was 0.5 mm, 0.5 mm and 10.5 mm, respectively. The effective pore sizes of the scaffold were 0.5 mm. The layer height of the print was set at 0.25 mm with two horizontal layers and two vertical layers to obtain 0.5 mm strut sizes. The printer nozzle was set at 220˚C with a travel speed of 150 mm/s and a z-axis travel speed of 23 mm/s. The infill density was kept at 100% and the infill layer height 0.25 mm and the pattern of infill was selected as linear. The resultant STL files were printed using the FDM system.

The plasma equipment (AutoGlow plasma system, Glow Research, USA) was operated at a set power of 50 W @ 100 kHz. The samples were suspended inside the plasma compartment, the vacuum pump was put in operation, and the plasma process was started once the pressure stabilized. Oxygen plasma was applied for varying times of 0–7 min, and the pressure was gradually increased from 0.08 Torr to a constant 0.47 Torr. The plasma-treated samples were immediately tested for hydrophilicity by measuring the sustained static contact angle. For the samples, typical plasma exposure times were set at 3, 5 and 7 min to assess the effect of treatment time. Another set of samples were similarly treated by argon plasma for comparison. In all cases three samples were each treated at the specified exposure times.

The PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P samples were tested for hydrophilicity by measuring their sustained contact angles (Krüss Drop Shape Analyser 4, Germany). Since porous structures cannot be used for measuring the contact angle (CA), thin uniform flat sheets of the samples were prepared for these measurements. The CA measurements for the samples were performed by the sessile drop technique at room temperature. The samples were mounted over a platform, and water drops were deposited over the polymer sample. Three measurements were conducted for each sample with replicates for quantifying wettability and average values have been reported.

The PLA and PLA-CD surfaces were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements using the ScanAsyst Bruker (USA) instrument via the tapping (intermittent contact) mode operation. The tip used was the NCHV-A model manufactured by Bruker, with a constant force of 42 N/m and a resonance frequency of 320 KHz. The average surface roughness was characterized as the Root Mean Square (Rrms) values based on the AFM z coordinate data profiles using equation (1), representing the average measure of peaks and valleys dotting the surface. The scanned surface areas were 5 μm × 5 μm and all experiments were performed in three replicates.
(1)
R
rms
=
r
1
2
+
r
2
2
+ ⋯ +
r
n
2
n

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characterization: FTIR and XPS measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were recorded using a Thermo Scientific spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, USA). The absorption spectra were analyzed for determining the characteristic functional groups of pure PLA, CDs and PLA-CD composites. To evaluate the surface compositions, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using the Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha + XPS spectrometer (USA). The spectra, acquired in the Constant Analyser Energy (CAE) mode, were analyzed over spatial ranges of 400 μm with a micro-focused monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.68 eV). An ultra-low energy, co-axial electron and Ar + ion beam was used for charge compensation. Photoelectron detection was achieved by a hemispherical analyzer at an angle of 90° to the sample surface. Survey scans, conducted on at least 3 randomly selected points, recorded in the range of 1350 – (−10) eV at pass energy of 200 eV, X-ray intensity of 12 keV and 6 mA, energy step size of 1 eV and dwell time of 0.01 s, were analyzed to characterize the surface chemistry. High-resolution C1s, O1s and N1s spectra were recorded using a pass energy of 50 eV, 0.05 s dwell time, and 0.1 eV energy step size to measure the specific types and amounts of the chemical compounds. The energy scale was calibrated to position the hydrocarbon component of the C1s spectrum at 285.0 eV, followed by curve fitting. Profile quantifications were conducted via Avantage™ software (version 5.99.0.0), and the chemical compositions were calculated with Wagner sensitivity factors after Shirley background subtraction.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) for the PLA and PLA-CD samples was performed using the Q5000 (TA Instruments, USA) for determining the thermal stability and degradation characteristics of the samples. Nitrogen was used as the purge gas at a flow rate of 20 ml.min−1, while the PLA and PLA-CD samples (5–10 mg) were heated at 30–800 0C at a rate of 10 0C.min−1. The characteristic thermal transitions and crystallinity characteristics of PLA and PLA-CD were determined using the Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) method with MDSC 2920 (TA Instruments, USA). About 10 mg samples were weighed and sealed hermetically with the Encapsulating Press (TA Instruments, USA) and heated at a rate of 10 ℃/min from room temperature to 220 ℃. The mechanical properties of the PLA and PLA-CD samples were tested on a universal testing machine (Instron 5567, USA). The stress–strain profiles and the sample yield strengths were measured.

Saos-2 (osteogenic sarcoma) cells were cultured in standard cell culture media comprising McCoy’s 5A medium (ATCC) supplemented with 15% fetal bovine serum (Sigma-Aldrich) and 1% antibiotic–antimycotic solution (Sigma-Aldrich) in T25 tissue culture flasks. Once the cells gained confluence, they were passaged and proliferated in T75 tissue culture flasks with the standard culture medium. On attaining confluency, the Saos-2 cells were subjected to trypsinization using trypsin (Sigma Aldrich). With the addition of standard culture medium, the effect of trypsin was neutralised and the trypsinized cells were collected in a sterile centrifuge tube. The obtained cell slurry was centrifuged at 1200 rpm for 6 mins. After centrifugation the supernatant was discarded but the cell pellet was mixed with a small amount of fresh medium. Cell count was performed of the obtained cell suspension.

The MTS proliferation assays were performed for 4, 8 and 12 days and the MTS/PMS solutions were added in required concentration. To the PMS/MTS solution, the culture media was added. For every 100 µl of media, 20 µl of MTS/PMS combined reagent was added. The ratio of culture medium to MTS/PMS solution was always maintained at 100 µl:20 µl respectively. The resultant mixture was added to individual wells of a fresh 24 well plate. Blank wells containing plain MTS/PMS reagent with culture media but without scaffolds were also prepared in triplicates. All samples including controls were done in triplicates. The scaffolds for MTS analysis were washed once in phosphate buffered saline and placed in the 24 well plate containing culture media with MTS/PMS solution. The samples were then placed in an incubator at 37˚C for 3 h. After 3 h, the well plates were observed for color development using a CLARIOstar microplate reader at 490 nm for spectrophotometric analysis. The reading at reference wavelength of 690 nm was later subtracted from the readings at 490 nm. The correction for absorbance at 690 nm was to eliminate background absorbance from fingerprint, cell debris and non-specific absorbance. Also, the readings of control wells were deducted. All experiments were performed in 3 replicates.

Saos-2 cells differentiation on PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P scaffolds were characterized by ALP analysis. Scaffolds were placed in 24 well plate and Saos-2 cells were seeded in contact with scaffolds at a concentration of 1.0x105 cells per scaffold in standard cell culture medium. After 4 days of cell inoculation the culture medium was replaced with osteogenic medium. The osteogenic medium was prepared by addition of 10 mM β -glycerophosphate and 50 µg/ml ascorbic acid to the standard culture medium. Saos-2 cells were cultured over a period of 1 and 2 weeks after the osteogenic induction. The osteogenic differentiation of Saos-2 cells for ALP observation was performed intracellularly.

Bone or ECM mineralization is evaluated by alizarin red S (ARS) dye. It is an anthraquinone dye and is useful to estimate calcium deposits from cultured cells. Scaffolds were placed in 24 well plate and Saos-2 cells were cells were seeded in contact with scaffolds at a concentration of 1.0x105 cells per scaffold in standard cell culture medium. After 4 days of cell inoculation the culture medium was replaced with osteogenic medium. The osteogenic medium was prepared by addition of 10 mM β -glycerophosphate and 50 µg/ml ascorbic acid to the standard culture medium. Saos-2 cells were cultured over a period of 4 weeks after osteogenic induction.

The incorporation of carbon quantum dots into the PLA polymer matrix produces composite scaffolds that are luminescent in nature due to the unique optical properties of the carbon dots (CDs). The down-converted PL emission spectra are caused by the optical selection of surface defect states near the Fermi level of CDs. With multi-photon mechanism, two or more photons are absorbed simultaneously, resulting in emission at a shorter wavelength relative to excitation – this is responsible for up-converted photoluminescence (PL) emission by CDs [24]. The PLA-CDs display two-photon excitation and support enhanced up- and down-conversion PL with increased depth profiling. With multi-photon excitation, PLA-CDs provide insights on specific intracellular organelles via fluorescence imaging [25]. The scaffolds based on PLA-CDs allow multi-photon assisted deep-tissue penetration by up-conversion, enabling use of longer NIR range wavelengths. Thus, bioimaging becomes more efficient due to enhanced penetration and reduced background auto-fluorescence [26].

UV–Vis spectroscopy absorption spectra of CDs demonstrated a minor peak at 230 nm and a prominent peak at 285 nm (Fig. S1, ESI). This is due to the π- π* transition in accordance to the transition of the polyaromatic chromophores of carbon (C = C) and also due to the n- π* transition of the carbonyl groups branching from the C-N, respectively. The remaining spectra from 400 to 500 nm can be referred to the remaining curcumin which is functionalized chemically on the carbon dot surface.

The carbon dots exhibited strong blue luminescence under 365 nm UV laser (Fig. S1, ESI). The photoluminescence spectra of the CDs (Fig. S2, ESI) showed that the excitation dependent emission bands shift from 390 to 600 nm when the excitation wavelength is 330 to 530 nm. The CD luminescence spectra exhibited down conversion emission contours. The chromophores provide fluorescence from the CDs enabling imaging of scaffolds and hence offers the opportunity for bioimaging of the associated cells. The PL spectra of CDs revealed a quantum yield (QY) of about 50% based on quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as standard [19]. Thus, the CDs have high potential as fluorogens for biomedical applications.

The PLA-CD pellets on melt blending with CDs were golden brown in color (Fig. S3A) as were the extruded filaments produced from the pellets (Fig. S3B). The filaments were defect-free, smooth in structure and exhibited blue luminescence under UV light (Fig. S3C). For 3D printing, a nozzle having 0.4 µm aperture was used with a layer height set to 0.25 µm using CAD software to produce 500 µm diameter struts with 500 µm spacing between the struts for the scaffolds. The 3D printed composite scaffolds (Fig. S4) from the PLA-CD filaments showed well-defined morphology and good mechanical stability.

The SEM images of the composite PLA-CD scaffolds with a 0/90° laydown pattern revealed that PLA-CD@P scaffolds had well-defined structures (Fig. S5A, ESI). The SEM images also indicated that the adhesion between the adjacent layers of the scaffold struts was satisfactory and that the inter-strut spacing between individual struts of 500 µm was regular in structure. The average pore sizes of about 500 µm ensured uniform spatial distribution and interconnectivity within the pore structure. The consistency of the optical results indicated that the CDs were relatively uniformly blended within the PLA matrix and the emission from PLA-CD composite was not affected by the FDM processing. The distribution of the nanoparticles using transmission electron microscopy (Fig. S5B, ESI) further showed that the CDs are reasonably well distributed within the polymer matrix.

Scaffolds prepared using hydrophobic polymers typically produce surfaces with poor wettability. The surface characteristics of such polymers and composites can be modified by treatment with abrasive chemicals or irradiation with charged particles or beam of photons. Such approaches modify the topology or surface chemistry, or both. In particular, the introduction of polar functional groups can enhance surface hydrophilicity, which can be characterized by measuring the liquid water contact angle at the substrate surface. Materials which possess super-hydrophilicity demonstrate spreading of liquid on a surface area that is much larger than the water droplet diameter. Moreover, the roughness of such surfaces favor capillary effects, whereby the water is disseminated within the channels over a large surface area, resulting in super-low contact angle. Certain functional groups (e.g., oxygen, hydroxyl, carboxyl groups) induced via plasma treatments can result in high wettability [27].

For contrast and comparison of hydrophilicity, we tested two types of plasma treatments on PLA and PLA-CD composies: argon (Ar) and oxygen (O2) based plasma. The argon plasma treatment over PLA-CD composites (Fig. 2 ) shows that after 7 min no significant change in contact angle is noticeable for the samples.

Contact angles of plasma treated samples were measured to assess the impact of oxygen plasma treatment of the composites over 0 – 7 min time duration. As shown in Fig. 3 , on subjecting the composite surface to plasma for 3 min the contact angle decreased from 72 to 43˚. While with 7 min plasma treatment, the contact angle was no longer measurable since the water drop was completely flat at 0˚. This confirmed that for our polymer composites, oxygen plasma treatment is a better option in generating hydrophilicity. Thus, superhydrophilicity was achieved with a modest regime of oxygen plasma exposure. This is possibly due to the coverage of the composite by oxygen and hydroxyl moieties which support strong hydrogen bonding and efficient water droplet accommodation. Oxygen plasma is known to present polar functional groups to treated surfaces [28] and lead to super-hydrophilic surfaces with low contact angles [29].

Table 1 shows a summary of the contact angle measurements for the base case of PLA and PLA-CD samples, and the results after their plasma treatments separately in argon and oxygen environments. We observe that for the PLA-CD samples subjected to oxygen plasma treatment, the material contact angle decreased to 0˚, demonstrating that superhydrophilicity was achieved after 7 min exposure.

These dramatic increases in roughness foreshadow the potential for obtaining superhydrophilicity of PLA-CD which was shown by the contact angle measurements. The oxygen plasma etching promotes the incorporation of oxygen as shown by the XPS measurements as well as modifying significantly the topography of the PLA-CD films (Table 2). The roughness caused by plasma etching in the nanometer dimension has also been reported elsewhere [30]. By functionalizing and enhancing roughness in the nanometer dimension by oxygen plasma, the film surfaces can be more reactive and hydrophilic. [31]. In an oxygen-containing discharge (like dry air), the main chemically reactive species are excited molecular oxygen species, atomic oxygen and ozone: these species induce the formation of oxygen-containing functionalities onto the PLA-CD surfaces [32]. Optimal increase in roughness result was obtained by using plasma exposure time of 7 min. Subsequently cell culture experiments were conducted to investigate the effects of plasma treatment on cell attachment and proliferation on the PLA and PLA-CD scaffolds.

The FTIR spectra (Fig. S6, ESI) shows an absorption peak at 3450 cm−1 for CDs and PLA-CDs spectra, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the H-bonds. These are due to the presence of the free –OH groups in the CDs. In addition, the C = C skeletal rings of the CDs are observed via their characteristic bands at wavenumbers of 1593 and 1512 cm−1. That these characteristic fingerprints are observed in both the pure CDs and the composite PLA-CD spectra indicate that the CDs have been incorporated into the polymer matrix and the two components have integrated satisfactorily in the composite structure.

The XPS spectra provided detailed insights into the elemental compositions before and after plasma treatment. The full XPS survey is given in Fig. 5 (A,B) and the surface elemental compositions are shown in Table 3 . To evaluate which functional groups are present on the surfaces before and after plasma exposure, high-resolution C1s were examined (Fig. 5 C,D). We note that plasma treated PLA-CD@P samples have enhanced oxygen (O1s) concentration and reduced carbon (C1s) concentration due to the influence of the O2-plasma. Table 3 shows quantitatively that the oxygen concentration increased by about 30% with a small increase in nitrogen concentration because of plasma activation on the film. These results indicate that the improved hydrophilicity of PLA-CD@P is due to the presence of enhanced O-based functional groups on the polymer surface [33,34]. Table 4 shows the underlying functional groups for the C1s profile, deconvoluted into three distinct peaks (Fig. 5 C,D) of 285.0 eV corresponding to C − C/C − H, 287.0 eV due to C = O, and 289.1 eV attributable to O − C = O. For the activated samples, increase in C = O peak at 287.0 eV and O-C = O peak at 289.1 eV and decrease in C–C peak at 284.9 eV are observed relative to untreated samples. Table 4 further shows increases in C = O and O-C = O functional groups due to plasma treatment by about 40% and 70%, respectively. Thus, these findings correlate well with the observed increase in hydrophilicity noted above and foreshadow the potential for increase in cellular affinity of PLA-CD@P due to the observed changes in the chemical composition due to oxygen plasma activation [30,35-37].

The TGA results (Fig. S7, ESI) show the measured temperatures for the degradation of the PLA and PLA-CD samples. The degradation onset temperature for the nanocomposite was enhanced by about 13 0C compared to the neat PLA and the decomposition of the material (about 100% mass loss) was obtained at 400 0C. These results confirm the integration of CDs into the PLA matrix with consequent enhanced thermal characteristics for the nanocomposite.

The DSC profiles (Fig. S8, ESI) show that the glass transition transformation of PLA took place at about 47.2 ℃ and two endothermic melting peaks were observed at 132.0 ℃ and 178.3 ℃. The occurrence of double melting peaks has been reported previously in semicrystalline polymers such as with PLA among others [6]. For the for PLA-CD composite, we found that the Tg value increased to 52.0 ℃, which is a significant rise of about 5 ℃ relative to neat PLA. This is due to the interactions between the nanoparticles and the PLA, leading to decrease in free volume and restriction of polymer chain movement. In addition, a cold crystallization was observed at 97.7 ℃ and a slight increase in melting temperature to 179.4 ℃ was noted for PLA-CD.

The stress–strain profiles (Fig. S9, ESI) we measured for PLA and PLA-CD samples show that the composite tensile strength has increased by about 24.1% through the addition of CDs to PLA in comparison to pure PLA. This is because of the enhanced surface area of the intercalated nanoparticles at the interfaces and the effective load transfer and dissipation at the interfaces between the CQDs and the PLA.

As a biopolymer, PLA has both favourable physicochemical and biological characteristics, which enable bespoke scaffold fabrication and eventual hydrolytic breakdown, ideal for biological applications. The ability of water to infiltrate into the polymer matrix determines the degree of PLA breakdown. PLA autocatalytic behaviour, pH, and temperature are the primary determinants of PLA degradation [49]. PLA can be hydrolysed in the presence of water to produce low-molecular weight water soluble oligomers and monomers. The molecular weight and crystallinity of biodegradable polyesters are important parameters in determining their hydrolytic breakdown [50].

PLA can also be degraded enzymatically by alcalase, lipase and esterase enzymes via ester bond cleavage. The activities of these enzymes are influenced by the chain stereochemistry, pH values, material crystallinity and operating temperature. PLA biodegradation in the environment is determined by the microorganisms present and operating conditions. The PLA surfaces are colonized first, followed by the release of extracellular enzymes, such as, proteases and lipases which hydrolyze the polymeric structure [51]. The CDs provide fast in vivo clearance efficiency without accumulation in organs and tissues, relative to invasive nanoparticles (such as, metal-based ones), which can induce organ damage. There has been no report on accumulation of CDs in any of the organs and tissues, and the use of PLA or CDs have not shown any significant adverse effects in biomedical applications [5,49,50,52-54]. Thus, we concentrated on investigating the biological characteristics of the PLA-CD@P samples in terms of their cell adhesion, cell proliferation, osteogenic differentiation, and extracellular matrix mineralization.

Bone tissue engineering potential requires the evaluation of cell polymer compatibility via assessment of cell proliferation on the scaffolds. Hence, 3D printed PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P scaffolds were tested for cell proliferation by MTS assays. Cell viability and proliferation over both types of composites were conducted using the standard growth medium (McCoy’s 5A medium supplemented with 15% FBS and 1% Antibiotic-Antimycotic). The cell viability of Saos-2 cells over PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P scaffolds were characterized by MTS assays over 4, 8 and 12 days. With time, the proliferation of Saos-2 cells over PLA-CD@P increased remarkably in comparison with PLA-CD. On day 4, the percentage increase in proliferation between PLA-CD@P and PLA-CD scaffold was about 55% and on day 12, the increase was 70%. These indicate that plasma treatment plays a crucial role in enhancing cell adhesion and growth that may potentially be scaled for in vivo application.

The results in Fig. 6 are consistent with the micrographs (Fig. S10, ESI) which show that the Saos-2 cells grew and proliferated over 12 days on both types of scaffolds. The response of Saos-2 cells over the PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P surface was facilitated by fixing and staining with ethanol and DAPI, respectively. On imaging the DAPI stained samples under the microscope, it was evident that the plasma coating improved the overall cellular proliferation and viability. As a result of DAPI staining, the Saos-2 cell nuclei were observed to be blue in color under the microscope. The scaffold porosity ensured that the cells are able to migrate across the scaffold and allow nutrient circulation, gaseous interchange, removal of excreta and cell-to-cell interactions, while preventing formation of localized cellular capsules [38].

Micrographs also indicate that for PLA-CD@P scaffolds, the cells spread across the pores and struts, and were well distributed across the scaffold in comparison with PLA-CD samples (Vid.(A,B), ESI: Videos show enhanced cell distribution on PLA-CD@P relative to PLA-CD). Cells were noted to have a higher proliferative state on the plasma treated composite and this trend kept on progressing till day 12. This is because on the treatment of composite surfaces with oxygen plasma, the surfaces became superhydrophilic. As the oxygen plasma etches the surface, its roughness is increased due to sputtering of the amorphous segments of the substrate [39]. The sub-micron level roughness induced is able to improve cell growth due to the morphological change of the substrate [40,41]. These along with the generation of functional groups account for increased osteoblast adhesion, cell proliferation and differentiation [42,43].

Alkaline phosphatase, an early stage biomarker for differentiation of osteoblasts [44], expresses itself in cultures during early stages of differentiation. It enhances the local phosphatase concentration matrix component and thus initiates the formation of hydroxyapatite [45,46]. The intracellular alkaline phosphatase activity was determined on PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P scaffolds on 1st and 2nd week after osteogenic induction (Fig. 7 ). Osteogenesis was triggered in Saos-2 cells by replacing the standard culture medium with osteogenic medium after 4 days of cell inoculation. The osteogenic medium was prepared by addition of 10 mM β -glycerophosphate and 50 µg/ml ascorbic acid to the standard culture medium. Results show that ALP activity was more in PLA-CD@P scaffolds in comparison with PLA-CD scaffolds. The percentage increase of ALP after osteogenic induction in PLA-CD@P and PLA-CD scaffolds was observed to be 37.82% for 1st week and 7.28% for 2nd week. Though the percentage increase of ALP got reduced from week 1 to week 2, the percentage was still higher for PLA-CD@P in comparison with PLA-CD scaffolds. The reduction in ALP could be because to the biphasic response demonstrated by alkaline phosphatase. ALP exhibits an increase in its expression during the early stages of osteoblast differentiation and exhibits a lowered response during the late phase of differentiation when mineralization initiates [46].

We used Alizarin red S staining to estimate ECM mineralization. ECM mineralization after osteogenic induction has been observed in cell cultures and calcium content increased with time, suggesting enhanced mineralization at later stages [47]. For proliferating osteoblasts, when cells are treated with β-glycerophosphate and ascorbic acid at confluency, the cells undergo a limited number of cell divisions and after ∼ 7 days exit the cell cycle. The presence of ascorbic acid enhances both the alkaline phosphatase expression as well as collagen synthesis. During the collagen matrix maturation stage, various proteins such as osteocalcin, bone sialoprotein and osteopontin accumulate along with collagen. Close to 14 days, the matrix vesicles are filled with a nucleation core of phosphate and calcium prior to the release of ECM. At ∼ 21 days, the matrix vesicles are released and within the collagen matrix, build-up of hydroxyapatite nodules account for mineralization. The ALP levels increase early in differentiation and through interaction with β-glycerophosphate produce increasing levels of inorganic phosphate in the ECM environment [48].

In our assays after osteogenic induction, ECM mineralization was estimated on week 3 and 4 for both PLA-CD and PLA-CD@P scaffolds. To induce osteogenesis, the growth media of Saos-2 cells was changed from standard growth media to osteogenic media 4 days after cell inoculation. Here, 10 mM β-glycerophosphate and 50 µg/ml ascorbic acid were introduced to the standard growth media in order to trigger osteogenesis. Mineralization results obtained over weeks 3 and 4 (Fig. 8 ) confirmed that the PLA-CD@P samples had enhanced calcium mineral deposits by about 70% relative to the PLA-CD samples, indicating increase in differentiated Saos-2 cells. Micrographs (Fig. S11, ESI) obtained by Alizarin red S staining at week 4, reveal that an increased number of cells have undergone ECM mineralization and enhanced mineralized nodule formation over PLA-CD@P compared with PLA-CD scaffolds.

We investigated the effects of plasma treatment on scaffolds produced from 3D printing of composites fabricated using poly(lactic acid) and 0.1 wt% carbon quantum dots (CDs). The key aim of this study was to determine the effects of composite treatment on cell proliferation and mineralization and whether the manipulation of hybrid scaffold surface chemistry affect the proliferative and mineralization properties. The main advantage of incorporating CDs within the PLA scaffold is to enhance bioimaging, since CDs could be traced and monitored by using excitation lasers of different wavelengths due to the photoluminescence of CDs. Current scaffolds do not offer the feature of facile tracking and in vivo imaging. The PLA-CD composite scaffolds were subjected to argon and oxygen plasma treatments separately, followed by surface hydrophilicity and surface chemistry measurements. Subsequent to our contact angle, FTIR and XPS characterizations, cellular interactions and osteogenesis were examined with Saos-2 cells. The oxygen plasma treatment was found to increase scaffold surface hydrophilicity with 0˚ contact angle being achieved after 7 min, demonstrating superhydrophilicity of sample. These results were found to be consistent with the surface chemistry determined by XPS, indicating the enhancement of surface moieties for enhanced cytocompatibility. The cell proliferation on the PLA-CD@P-O2 was found to increase remarkably with enhanced ECM mineralization. These results confirmed that the quantum dots and plasma treatment played an important role in improving osteoblast like cell adhesion and functions. Thus, oxygen plasma treatment of PLA scaffold holds significant potential for tissue engineering, while the CD-based hybrid PLA composite opens opportunities for built-in scanning and monitoring of the scaffold and cellular environment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests which may be considered as potential competing interests: The authors KD, ZM and VG are co-inventors on a patent application (WO PCT/EP2021/080109) titled “Methods and composition thereof of super hydrophile bioresorbable polymer composites for real time monitoring of scaffolds for tissue engineering” filed on 29 October, 2021. Author VG is a named inventor on an Australian Patent Application No. 2020901760 titled “Nanoparticle conjugates and their use in the therapeutic applications” filed on 29th May 2020.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the polymer raw materials provided by Evonik Industries GmbH. VG acknowledges the support from an ARC (Australian Research Council) Linkage grant received for this project. KD acknowledges Dr M. Hassan and A. Nasrin for sharing the synthesis procedure for CDs. The use of laboratory facilities of Bosch Institute and the Nano Institute (The University of Sydney) are gratefully acknowledged. KD acknowledges a scholarship from the Linkage grant and a Nano Institute PG supplementary scholarship.
